revision.text:
'''陶器千塚古墳群'''（とうきせんずかこふんぐん）は、[[大阪府]][[堺市]]中区東山、陶器北、平井に分布する[[古墳時代]]後期の[[群集墳]]である。現在ではその多くは消滅している。
== 概要== 陶器千塚古墳群は明治時代に開墾によって相当数が破壊されている。それでも昭和２０年代(１９４５年～１９５４年)には小型の前方後円墳を含む３５基が現存し、その大部分は円墳であった。木棺直葬の葬方をとり、ツキ塚のように２棺が併置きされたものもあった。出土遺物には、小型銅鏡、玉類、鉄刀、須恵器、土師器などがあり、一基には馬具を副葬していた。また、須恵器の蓋付の杯の中にカヤの実を入れた例があった。古墳時代後期初頭から形成が始まり、後期後半に形成を終えている。なお陶器千塚にはカマド塚と命名された火葬古墳がある。窯形木心粘土槨とでも呼ぶべき埋葬施設を築き、この内部で遺体を火葬にし、その後火葬にした骨を動かすことなく、墳丘を築いたもので、６世紀末から７世紀初頭の時期のもののようである。カマド塚と言う名称は陶器千塚のこの火葬古墳の固有名称としてつけられものであるが、その後、この種の類例に一般化して使われるようになったものである。陶器千塚は、火葬という改新的葬方が、日本列島においてひときわ早く出現したところであり、ここにこの古墳群を築いた集団の特色がうかがえようである。
==参考文献 == 森浩一「陶器千塚とカマド塚」 『大阪府史 第一巻』 大阪府史編纂委員会 １９７８年 ９１１項～９１２項 [[category:古墳]]
[[category:大阪府の古墳]]
[[category:堺市の歴史]]

==参考文献 == 森浩一「陶器千塚とカマド塚」 『大阪府史 第一巻』 大阪府史編纂委員会 １９７８年 ９１１項～９１２項 [[category:古墳]]
[[category:大阪府の古墳]]
[[category:堺市の歴史]]

'''陶器千塚古墳群'''（とうきせんづかこふんぐん）は、[[大阪府]][[堺市]][[中区 (堺市)|中区]]東山、陶器北、平井に分布する[[古墳時代]]後期の[[群集墳]]である。現在ではその多くは消滅している。
== 概要== 陶器千塚古墳群は明治時代に開墾によって相当数が破壊されている。それでも昭和20年代（1945年～1954年）には小型の前方後円墳を含む35基が現存し、その大部分は円墳であった。木棺直葬の葬方をとり、ツキ塚のように2棺が併置きされたものもあった。出土遺物には、小型銅鏡、玉類、鉄刀、須恵器、土師器などがあり、一基には馬具を副葬していた。また、須恵器の蓋付の杯の中にカヤの実を入れた例があった。古墳時代後期初頭から形成が始まり、後期後半に形成を終えている。なお陶器千塚にはカマド塚と命名された火葬古墳がある。窯形木心粘土槨とでも呼ぶべき埋葬施設を築き、この内部で遺体を火葬にし、その後火葬にした骨を動かすことなく、墳丘を築いたもので、6世紀末から７世紀初頭の時期のもののようである。カマド塚と言う名称は陶器千塚のこの火葬古墳の固有名称としてつけられものであるが、その後、この種の類例に一般化して使われるようになったものである。陶器千塚は、火葬という改新的葬方が、日本列島においてひときわ早く出現したところであり、ここにこの古墳群を築いた集団の特色がうかがえるようである。
==参考文献 == 森浩一「陶器千塚とカマド塚」 『大阪府史 第一巻』 大阪府史編纂委員会 1978年 911項～912項

==参考文献 == *森浩一「陶器千塚とカマド塚」 『大阪府史 第一巻』 大阪府史編纂委員会 1978年 911項～912項
==関連項目 == *[[信太千塚古墳群]]
== 外部リンク== [http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/city/_bunkazai/_maibun/ntouki-sen29.htm 陶器千塚29号墳] 堺の遺跡紹介 堺市
{{デフォルトソート:とうきせんつかこふんくん}} [[category:群集墳]] [[category:大阪府の古墳]] [[category:堺市の歴史]] [[Category:中区 (堺市)]]

revision.comment: Created page with "'''Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group''' (陶器千塚古墳群) == Overview == The Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group is a collection of [[kofun]] (ancient burial mounds) from the late [[Kofun period]], located in [[Higashiyama]], [[Tōki Kita]], and [[Hirai]] in [[Naka Ward]], [[Sakai City]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. Many of the burial mounds have been destroyed over time. During the [[Meiji period]], numerous mounds were destroyed through land clearing. However, as of the [[Shōwa era]]..."
revision.text:
'''Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group''' (陶器千塚古墳群)
== Overview == The Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group is a collection of [[kofun]] (ancient burial mounds) from the late [[Kofun period]], located in [[Higashiyama]], [[Tōki Kita]], and [[Hirai]] in [[Naka Ward]], [[Sakai City]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. Many of the burial mounds have been destroyed over time.
During the [[Meiji period]], numerous mounds were destroyed through land clearing. However, as of the [[Shōwa era]] (1945–1954), 35 mounds remained, including small [[zenpō-kōen-fun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and predominantly [[empun]] (round mounds). These mounds employed direct burial of wooden coffins, with some, such as Tsuki-zuka, containing two coffins placed adjacently. Excavated artifacts include small bronze mirrors, beads, iron swords, [[Sue ware]], and [[Haji ware]]. One mound included horse harnesses as burial goods, while another example included nuts of the Japanese nutmeg tree placed in a lidded Sue ware dish.
The group is believed to have been formed during the early to late stages of the late Kofun period. Of note is the Kamado-zuka mound, named for its innovative fire burial method. This burial practice, which used a kiln-shaped clay-lined burial chamber, involved cremating the body within the chamber and constructing the mound over the remains without relocating the bones. This practice dates to the late 6th to early 7th century. Although initially a unique name for the Kamado-zuka mound in Tōki Senzuka, the term later became a generalized descriptor for similar examples.
The Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group stands out for introducing cremation—a revolutionary burial method in the Japanese archipelago—highlighting the unique characteristics of the community that created these burial mounds.
== References == * Mori Kōichi, "Tōki Senzuka and Kamado-zuka," *Osaka-fu Shi, Volume 1,* Osaka Prefecture History Compilation Committee, 1978, pp. 911–912.
== Related Articles == * [[Shinoda Senzuka Kofun Group]]
== External Links == * [http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/city/_bunkazai/_maibun/ntouki-sen29.htm Tōki Senzuka 29th Kofun] - Sakai City Archaeological Site Introduction
{{DEFAULTSORT:Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group}} [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:Clustered burial mounds]] [[Category:History of Naka Ward, Sakai]] [[Category:History of Osaka Prefecture]]

These mounds employed direct burial of wooden coffins, with some, such as Tsuki-zuka, containing two coffins placed adjacently. Excavated artifacts include small bronze mirrors, beads, iron swords, [[Sue ware]], and [[Haji ware]]. One mound included horse harnesses as burial goods, while another example included nuts of the Japanese nutmeg tree placed in a lidded Sue ware dish.
The group is believed to have been formed during the early to late stages of the late Kofun period. Of note is the Kamado-zuka mound, named for its innovative fire burial method. This burial practice, which used a kiln-shaped clay-lined burial chamber, involved cremating the body within the chamber and constructing the mound over the remains without relocating the bones. This practice dates to the late 6th to early 7th century. Although initially a unique name for the Kamado-zuka mound in Tōki Senzuka, the term later became a generalized descriptor for similar examples.
The Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group stands out for introducing cremation—a revolutionary burial method in the Japanese archipelago—highlighting the unique characteristics of the community that created these burial mounds.
== References == * Mori Kōichi, "Tōki Senzuka and Kamado-zuka," *Osaka-fu Shi, Volume 1,* Osaka Prefecture History Compilation Committee, 1978, pp. 911–912.

The group is believed to have been formed during the early to late stages of the late Kofun period. Of note is the Kamado-zuka mound, named for its innovative fire burial method. This burial practice, which used a kiln-shaped clay-lined burial chamber, involved cremating the body within the chamber and constructing the mound over the remains without relocating the bones. This practice dates to the late 6th to early 7th century. Although initially a unique name for the Kamado-zuka mound in Tōki Senzuka, the term later became a generalized descriptor for similar examples.
The Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group stands out for introducing cremation—a revolutionary burial method in the Japanese archipelago—highlighting the unique characteristics of the community that created these burial mounds.

'''Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group''' (陶器千塚古墳群) [[ja:%E9%99%B6%E5%99%A8%E5%8D%83%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3%E7%BE%A4]] is a collection of [[kofun]] (ancient burial mounds) from the late [[Kofun period]], located in [[Higashiyama]], [[Tōki Kita]], and [[Hirai]] in [[Naka Ward]], [[Sakai City]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. Many of the burial mounds have been destroyed over time.
During the [[Meiji period]], numerous mounds were destroyed through land clearing. However, as of the [[Shōwa era]] (1945–1954), 35 mounds remained, including small [[zenpō-kōen-fun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and predominantly [[empun]] (round mounds). These mounds employed direct burial of wooden coffins, with some, such as Tsuki-zuka, containing two coffins placed adjacently. Excavated artifacts include small bronze mirrors, beads, iron swords, [[Sue ware]], and [[Haji ware]]. One mound included horse harnesses as burial goods, while another example included nuts of the Japanese nutmeg tree placed in a lidded Sue ware dish.
The group is believed to have been formed during the early to late stages of the late Kofun period. Of note is the Kamado-zuka mound, named for its innovative fire burial method. This burial practice, which used a kiln-shaped clay-lined burial chamber, involved cremating the body within the chamber and constructing the mound over the remains without relocating the bones. This practice dates to the late 6th to early 7th century. Although initially a unique name for the Kamado-zuka mound in Tōki Senzuka, the term later became a generalized descriptor for similar examples.
The Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group stands out for introducing cremation—a revolutionary burial method in the Japanese archipelago—highlighting the unique characteristics of the community that created these burial mounds.
== References == * Mori Kōichi, "Tōki Senzuka and Kamado-zuka," *Osaka-fu Shi, Volume 1,* Osaka Prefecture History Compilation Committee, 1978, pp. 911–912.

'''Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group''' (陶器千塚古墳群) [[ja:%E9%99%B6%E5%99%A8%E5%8D%83%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3%E7%BE%A4]] is a collection of [[kofun]] (ancient burial mounds) from the late [[Kofun period]], located in [[Higashiyama]], [[Tōki Kita]], and [[Hirai]] in [[Naka Ward]], [[Sakai City]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. Many of the burial mounds have been destroyed over time.
During the [[Meiji period]], numerous mounds were destroyed through land clearing. However, as of the [[Shōwa era]] (1945–1954), 35 mounds remained, including small [[zenpō-kōen-fun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and predominantly [[empun]] (round mounds). These mounds employed direct burial of wooden coffins, with some, such as Tsuki-zuka, containing two coffins placed adjacently. Excavated artifacts include small bronze mirrors, beads, iron swords, [[Sue ware]], and [[Haji ware]]. One mound included horse harnesses as burial goods, while another example included nuts of the Japanese nutmeg tree placed in a lidded Sue ware dish.
The group is believed to have been formed during the early to late stages of the late Kofun period. Of note is the Kamado-zuka mound, named for its innovative fire burial method. This burial practice, which used a kiln-shaped clay-lined burial chamber, involved cremating the body within the chamber and constructing the mound over the remains without relocating the bones. This practice dates to the late 6th to early 7th century. Although initially a unique name for the Kamado-zuka mound in Tōki Senzuka, the term later became a generalized descriptor for similar examples.
The Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group stands out for introducing cremation—a revolutionary burial method in the Japanese archipelago—highlighting the unique characteristics of the community that created these burial mounds.

'''Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group''' (陶器千塚古墳群) [[ja:%E9%99%B6%E5%99%A8%E5%8D%83%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3%E7%BE%A4]] is a collection of [[kofun]] (ancient burial mounds) from the late [[Kofun period]], located in [[Higashiyama]], [[Tōki Kita]], and [[Hirai]] in [[Naka Ward]], [[Sakai City]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. Many of the burial mounds have been destroyed over time.
During the [[Meiji period]], numerous mounds were destroyed through land clearing. However, as of the [[Shōwa era]] (1945–1954), 35 mounds remained, including small [[zenpō-kōen-fun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and predominantly [[empun]] (round mounds). These mounds employed direct burial of wooden coffins, with some, such as Tsuki-zuka, containing two coffins placed adjacently. Excavated artifacts include small bronze mirrors, beads, iron swords, [[Sue ware]], and [[Haji ware]]. One mound included horse harnesses as burial goods, while another example included nuts of the Japanese nutmeg tree placed in a lidded Sue ware dish.
The group is believed to have been formed during the early to late stages of the late Kofun period. Of note is the Kamado-zuka mound, named for its innovative fire burial method. This burial practice, which used a kiln-shaped clay-lined burial chamber, involved cremating the body within the chamber and constructing the mound over the remains without relocating the bones. This practice dates to the late 6th to early 7th century. Although initially a unique name for the Kamado-zuka mound in Tōki Senzuka, the term later became a generalized descriptor for similar examples.
The Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group stands out for introducing cremation—a revolutionary burial method in the Japanese archipelago—highlighting the unique characteristics of the community that created these burial mounds.
== References == * Mori Kōichi, "Tōki Senzuka and Kamado-zuka," *Osaka-fu Shi, Volume 1,* Osaka Prefecture History Compilation Committee, 1978, pp. 911–912.

'''Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group''' (陶器千塚古墳群) [[ja:%E9%99%B6%E5%99%A8%E5%8D%83%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3%E7%BE%A4]] is a collection of [[kofun]] (ancient burial mounds) from the late [[Kofun period]], located in [[Higashiyama]], [[Tōki Kita]], and [[Hirai]] in [[Naka Ward]], [[Sakai City]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. Many of the burial mounds have been destroyed over time.
During the [[Meiji period]], numerous mounds were destroyed through land clearing. However, as of the [[Shōwa era]] (1945–1954), 35 mounds remained, including small [[zenpō-kōen-fun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and predominantly [[empun]] (round mounds). These mounds employed direct burial of wooden coffins, with some, such as Tsuki-zuka, containing two coffins placed adjacently. Excavated artifacts include small bronze mirrors, beads, iron swords, [[Sue ware]], and [[Haji ware]]. One mound included horse harnesses as burial goods, while another example included nuts of the Japanese nutmeg tree placed in a lidded Sue ware dish.
The group is believed to have been formed during the early to late stages of the late Kofun period. Of note is the Kamado-zuka mound, named for its innovative fire burial method. This burial practice, which used a kiln-shaped clay-lined burial chamber, involved cremating the body within the chamber and constructing the mound over the remains without relocating the bones. This practice dates to the late 6th to early 7th century. Although initially a unique name for the Kamado-zuka mound in Tōki Senzuka, the term later became a generalized descriptor for similar examples.
The Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group stands out for introducing cremation—a revolutionary burial method in the Japanese archipelago—highlighting the unique characteristics of the community that created these burial mounds.

Although initially a unique name for the Kamado-zuka mound in Tōki Senzuka, the term later became a generalized descriptor for similar examples.
The Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group stands out for introducing cremation—a revolutionary burial method in the Japanese archipelago—highlighting the unique characteristics of the community that created these burial mounds.

'''Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group''' (陶器千塚古墳群) [[ja:%E9%99%B6%E5%99%A8%E5%8D%83%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3%E7%BE%A4]] is a collection of [[kofun]] (ancient burial mounds) from the late [[Kofun period]], located in [[Higashiyama]], [[Tōki Kita]], and [[Hirai]] in [[Naka Ward]], [[Sakai City]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. Many of the burial mounds have been destroyed over time.
During the [[Meiji period]], numerous mounds were destroyed through land clearing. However, as of the [[Shōwa era]] (1945–1954), 35 mounds remained, including small [[zenpō-kōen-fun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and predominantly [[empun]] (round mounds). These mounds employed direct burial of wooden coffins, with some, such as Tsuki-zuka, containing two coffins placed adjacently. Excavated artifacts include small bronze mirrors, beads, iron swords, [[Sue ware]], and [[Haji ware]]. One mound included horse harnesses as burial goods, while another example included nuts of the Japanese nutmeg tree placed in a lidded Sue ware dish.
The group is believed to have been formed during the early to late stages of the late Kofun period. Of note is the Kamado-zuka mound, named for its innovative fire burial method. This burial practice, which used a kiln-shaped clay-lined burial chamber, involved cremating the body within the chamber and constructing the mound over the remains without relocating the bones. This practice dates to the late 6th to early 7th century. Although initially a unique name for the Kamado-zuka mound in Tōki Senzuka, the term later became a generalized descriptor for similar examples.
The Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group stands out for introducing cremation—a revolutionary burial method in the Japanese archipelago—highlighting the unique characteristics of the community that created these burial mounds.

The Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group stands out for introducing cremation—a revolutionary burial method in the Japanese archipelago—highlighting the unique characteristics of the community that created these burial mounds.

'''Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group''' (陶器千塚古墳群) [[ja:%E9%99%B6%E5%99%A8%E5%8D%83%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3%E7%BE%A4]] is a collection of [[kofun]] (ancient burial mounds) from the late [[Kofun period]], located in [[Higashiyama]], [[Tōki Kita]], and [[Hirai]] in [[Naka Ward]], [[Sakai City]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. Many of the burial mounds have been destroyed over time.
During the [[Meiji period]], numerous mounds were destroyed through land clearing. However, as of the [[Shōwa era]] (1945–1954), 35 mounds remained, including small [[zenpō-kōen-fun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and predominantly [[empun]] (round mounds). These mounds employed direct burial of wooden coffins, with some, such as Tsuki-zuka, containing two coffins placed adjacently. Excavated artifacts include small bronze mirrors, beads, iron swords, [[Sue ware]], and [[Haji ware]]. One mound included horse harnesses as burial goods, while another example included nuts of the Japanese nutmeg tree placed in a lidded Sue ware dish.
The group is believed to have been formed during the early to late stages of the late Kofun period. Of note is the Kamado-zuka mound, named for its innovative fire burial method. This burial practice, which used a kiln-shaped clay-lined burial chamber, involved cremating the body within the chamber and constructing the mound over the remains without relocating the bones. This practice dates to the late 6th to early 7th century. Although initially a unique name for the Kamado-zuka mound in Tōki Senzuka, the term later became a generalized descriptor for similar examples.
The Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group stands out for introducing cremation—a revolutionary burial method in the Japanese archipelago—highlighting the unique characteristics of the community that created these burial mounds.
== References == * Mori Kōichi, "Tōki Senzuka and Kamado-zuka," *Osaka-fu Shi, Volume 1,* Osaka Prefecture History Compilation Committee, 1978, pp. 911–912.

'''Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group''' (陶器千塚古墳群) [[ja:%E9%99%B6%E5%99%A8%E5%8D%83%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3%E7%BE%A4]] is a collection of [[kofun]] (ancient burial mounds) from the late [[Kofun period]], located in [[Higashiyama]], [[Tōki Kita]], and [[Hirai]] in [[Naka Ward]], [[Sakai City]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. Many of the burial mounds have been destroyed over time.
During the [[Meiji period]], numerous mounds were destroyed through land clearing. However, as of the [[Shōwa era]] (1945–1954), 35 mounds remained, including small [[zenpō-kōen-fun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and predominantly [[empun]] (round mounds). These mounds employed direct burial of wooden coffins, with some, such as Tsuki-zuka, containing two coffins placed adjacently. Excavated artifacts include small bronze mirrors, beads, iron swords, [[Sue ware]], and [[Haji ware]]. One mound included horse harnesses as burial goods, while another example included nuts of the Japanese nutmeg tree placed in a lidded Sue ware dish.
The group is believed to have been formed during the early to late stages of the late Kofun period. Of note is the Kamado-zuka mound, named for its innovative fire burial method. This burial practice, which used a kiln-shaped clay-lined burial chamber, involved cremating the body within the chamber and constructing the mound over the remains without relocating the bones. This practice dates to the late 6th to early 7th century. Although initially a unique name for the Kamado-zuka mound in Tōki Senzuka, the term later became a generalized descriptor for similar examples.
The Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group stands out for introducing cremation—a revolutionary burial method in the Japanese archipelago—highlighting the unique characteristics of the community that created these burial mounds.
== References == * Mori Kōichi, "Tōki Senzuka and Kamado-zuka," *Osaka-fu Shi, Volume 1,* Osaka Prefecture History Compilation Committee, 1978, pp. 911–912.

'''Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group''' (陶器千塚古墳群) [[ja:%E9%99%B6%E5%99%A8%E5%8D%83%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3%E7%BE%A4]] is a collection of [[kofun]] (ancient burial mounds) from the late [[Kofun period]], located in [[Higashiyama]], [[Tōki Kita]], and [[Hirai]] in [[Naka Ward]], [[Sakai City]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. Many of the burial mounds have been destroyed over time.
During the [[Meiji period]], numerous mounds were destroyed through land clearing. However, as of the [[Shōwa era]] (1945–1954), 35 mounds remained, including small [[zenpō-kōen-fun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and predominantly [[empun]] (round mounds). These mounds employed direct burial of wooden coffins, with some, such as Tsuki-zuka, containing two coffins placed adjacently. Excavated artifacts include small bronze mirrors, beads, iron swords, [[Sue ware]], and [[Haji ware]]. One mound included horse harnesses as burial goods, while another example included nuts of the Japanese nutmeg tree placed in a lidded Sue ware dish.
The group is believed to have been formed during the early to late stages of the late Kofun period. Of note is the Kamado-zuka mound, named for its innovative fire burial method. This burial practice, which used a kiln-shaped clay-lined burial chamber, involved cremating the body within the chamber and constructing the mound over the remains without relocating the bones. This practice dates to the late 6th to early 7th century. Although initially a unique name for the Kamado-zuka mound in Tōki Senzuka, the term later became a generalized descriptor for similar examples.
The Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group stands out for introducing cremation—a revolutionary burial method in the Japanese archipelago—highlighting the unique characteristics of the community that created these burial mounds.
== References == * Mori Kōichi, "Tōki Senzuka and Kamado-zuka," *Osaka-fu Shi, Volume 1,* Osaka Prefecture History Compilation Committee, 1978, pp. 911–912.

However, as of the [[Shōwa era]] (1945–1954), 35 mounds remained, including small [[zenpō-kōen-fun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and predominantly [[empun]] (round mounds). These mounds employed direct burial of wooden coffins, with some, such as Tsuki-zuka, containing two coffins placed adjacently. Excavated artifacts include small bronze mirrors, beads, iron swords, [[Sue ware]], and [[Haji ware]]. One mound included horse harnesses as burial goods, while another example included nuts of the Japanese nutmeg tree placed in a lidded Sue ware dish.
The group is believed to have been formed during the early to late stages of the late Kofun period. Of note is the Kamado-zuka mound, named for its innovative fire burial method. This burial practice, which used a kiln-shaped clay-lined burial chamber, involved cremating the body within the chamber and constructing the mound over the remains without relocating the bones. This practice dates to the late 6th to early 7th century. Although initially a unique name for the Kamado-zuka mound in Tōki Senzuka, the term later became a generalized descriptor for similar examples.
The Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group stands out for introducing cremation—a revolutionary burial method in the Japanese archipelago—highlighting the unique characteristics of the community that created these burial mounds.

'''Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group''' (陶器千塚古墳群) [[ja:%E9%99%B6%E5%99%A8%E5%8D%83%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3%E7%BE%A4]] is a collection of [[kofun]] (ancient burial mounds) from the late [[Kofun period]], located in [[Higashiyama]], [[Tōki Kita]], and [[Hirai]] in [[Naka Ward]], [[Sakai City]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. Many of the burial mounds have been destroyed over time.
During the [[Meiji period]], numerous mounds were destroyed through land clearing. However, as of the [[Shōwa era]] (1945–1954), 35 mounds remained, including small [[zenpō-kōen-fun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and predominantly [[empun]] (round mounds). These mounds employed direct burial of wooden coffins, with some, such as Tsuki-zuka, containing two coffins placed adjacently. Excavated artifacts include small bronze mirrors, beads, iron swords, [[Sue ware]], and [[Haji ware]]. One mound included horse harnesses as burial goods, while another example included nuts of the Japanese nutmeg tree placed in a lidded Sue ware dish.
The group is believed to have been formed during the early to late stages of the late Kofun period. Of note is the Kamado-zuka mound, named for its innovative fire burial method. This burial practice, which used a kiln-shaped clay-lined burial chamber, involved cremating the body within the chamber and constructing the mound over the remains without relocating the bones. This practice dates to the late 6th to early 7th century. Although initially a unique name for the Kamado-zuka mound in Tōki Senzuka, the term later became a generalized descriptor for similar examples.
The Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group stands out for introducing cremation—a revolutionary burial method in the Japanese archipelago—highlighting the unique characteristics of the community that created these burial mounds.
== References == * Mori Kōichi, "Tōki Senzuka and Kamado-zuka," *Osaka-fu Shi, Volume 1,* Osaka Prefecture History Compilation Committee, 1978, pp. 911–912.

'''Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group''' (陶器千塚古墳群) [[ja:%E9%99%B6%E5%99%A8%E5%8D%83%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3%E7%BE%A4]] is a collection of [[kofun]] (ancient burial mounds) from the late [[Kofun period]], located in [[Higashiyama]], [[Tōki Kita]], and [[Hirai]] in [[Naka Ward]], [[Sakai City]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. Many of the burial mounds have been destroyed over time.
During the [[Meiji period]], numerous mounds were destroyed through land clearing. However, as of the [[Shōwa era]] (1945–1954), 35 mounds remained, including small [[zenpō-kōen-fun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and predominantly [[empun]] (round mounds). These mounds employed direct burial of wooden coffins, with some, such as Tsuki-zuka, containing two coffins placed adjacently. Excavated artifacts include small bronze mirrors, beads, iron swords, [[Sue ware]], and [[Haji ware]]. One mound included horse harnesses as burial goods, while another example included nuts of the Japanese nutmeg tree placed in a lidded Sue ware dish.
The group is believed to have been formed during the early to late stages of the late Kofun period. Of note is the Kamado-zuka mound, named for its innovative fire burial method. This burial practice, which used a kiln-shaped clay-lined burial chamber, involved cremating the body within the chamber and constructing the mound over the remains without relocating the bones. This practice dates to the late 6th to early 7th century. Although initially a unique name for the Kamado-zuka mound in Tōki Senzuka, the term later became a generalized descriptor for similar examples.
The Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group stands out for introducing cremation—a revolutionary burial method in the Japanese archipelago—highlighting the unique characteristics of the community that created these burial mounds.

revision.text:
'''Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group''' (陶器千塚古墳群) [[ja:%E9%99%B6%E5%99%A8%E5%8D%83%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3%E7%BE%A4]] is a collection of [[kofun]] (ancient burial mounds) from the late [[Kofun period]], located in [[Higashiyama]], [[Tōki Kita]], and [[Hirai]] in [[Naka Ward]], [[Sakai City]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. Many of the burial mounds have been destroyed over time.
During the [[Meiji period]], numerous mounds were destroyed through land clearing. However, as of the [[Shōwa era]] (1945–1954), 35 mounds remained, including small [[zenpō-kōen-fun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and predominantly [[empun]] (round mounds). These mounds employed direct burial of wooden coffins, with some, such as Tsuki-zuka, containing two coffins placed adjacently. Excavated artifacts include small bronze mirrors, beads, iron swords, [[Sue ware]], and [[Haji ware]]. One mound included horse harnesses as burial goods, while another example included nuts of the Japanese nutmeg tree placed in a lidded Sue ware dish.
The group is believed to have been formed during the early to late stages of the late Kofun period. Of note is the Kamado-zuka mound, named for its innovative fire burial method. This burial practice, which used a kiln-shaped clay-lined burial chamber, involved cremating the body within the chamber and constructing the mound over the remains without relocating the bones. This practice dates to the late 6th to early 7th century. Although initially a unique name for the Kamado-zuka mound in Tōki Senzuka, the term later became a generalized descriptor for similar examples.
The Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group stands out for introducing cremation—a revolutionary burial method in the Japanese archipelago—highlighting the unique characteristics of the community that created these burial mounds.
== References == * Mori Kōichi, "Tōki Senzuka and Kamado-zuka," *Osaka-fu Shi, Volume 1,* Osaka Prefecture History Compilation Committee, 1978, pp. 911–912.

'''Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group''' (陶器千塚古墳群) [[ja:%E9%99%B6%E5%99%A8%E5%8D%83%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3%E7%BE%A4]] is a collection of [[kofun]] (ancient burial mounds) from the late [[Kofun period]], located in [[Higashiyama]], [[Tōki Kita]], and [[Hirai]] in [[Naka Ward]], [[Sakai City]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. Many of the burial mounds have been destroyed over time.
During the [[Meiji period]], numerous mounds were destroyed through land clearing. However, as of the [[Shōwa era]] (1945–1954), 35 mounds remained, including small [[zenpō-kōen-fun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and predominantly [[empun]] (round mounds). These mounds employed direct burial of wooden coffins, with some, such as Tsuki-zuka, containing two coffins placed adjacently. Excavated artifacts include small bronze mirrors, beads, iron swords, [[Sue ware]], and [[Haji ware]]. One mound included horse harnesses as burial goods, while another example included nuts of the Japanese nutmeg tree placed in a lidded Sue ware dish.
The group is believed to have been formed during the early to late stages of the late Kofun period. Of note is the Kamado-zuka mound, named for its innovative fire burial method. This burial practice, which used a kiln-shaped clay-lined burial chamber, involved cremating the body within the chamber and constructing the mound over the remains without relocating the bones. This practice dates to the late 6th to early 7th century. Although initially a unique name for the Kamado-zuka mound in Tōki Senzuka, the term later became a generalized descriptor for similar examples.
The Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group stands out for introducing cremation—a revolutionary burial method in the Japanese archipelago—highlighting the unique characteristics of the community that created these burial mounds.

'''Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group''' (陶器千塚古墳群) [[ja:%E9%99%B6%E5%99%A8%E5%8D%83%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3%E7%BE%A4]] is a collection of [[kofun]] (ancient burial mounds) from the late [[Kofun period]], located in [[Higashiyama]], [[Tōki Kita]], and [[Hirai]] in [[Naka Ward]], [[Sakai City]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. Many of the burial mounds have been destroyed over time.
During the [[Meiji period]], numerous mounds were destroyed through land clearing. However, as of the [[Shōwa era]] (1945–1954), 35 mounds remained, including small [[zenpō-kōen-fun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and predominantly [[empun]] (round mounds). These mounds employed direct burial of wooden coffins, with some, such as Tsuki-zuka, containing two coffins placed adjacently. Excavated artifacts include small bronze mirrors, beads, iron swords, [[Sue ware]], and [[Haji ware]]. One mound included horse harnesses as burial goods, while another example included nuts of the Japanese nutmeg tree placed in a lidded Sue ware dish.
The group is believed to have been formed during the early to late stages of the late Kofun period. Of note is the Kamado-zuka mound, named for its innovative fire burial method. This burial practice, which used a kiln-shaped clay-lined burial chamber, involved cremating the body within the chamber and constructing the mound over the remains without relocating the bones. This practice dates to the late 6th to early 7th century. Although initially a unique name for the Kamado-zuka mound in Tōki Senzuka, the term later became a generalized descriptor for similar examples.
The Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group stands out for introducing cremation—a revolutionary burial method in the Japanese archipelago—highlighting the unique characteristics of the community that created these burial mounds.

Many of the burial mounds have been destroyed over time.

However, as of the [[Shōwa era]] (1945–1954), 35 mounds remained, including small [[zenpō-kōen-fun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and predominantly [[empun]] (round mounds). These mounds employed direct burial of wooden coffins, with some, such as Tsuki-zuka, containing two coffins placed adjacently. Excavated artifacts include small bronze mirrors, beads, iron swords, [[Sue ware]], and [[Haji ware]]. One mound included horse harnesses as burial goods, while another example included nuts of the Japanese nutmeg tree placed in a lidded Sue ware dish.
The group is believed to have been formed during the early to late stages of the late Kofun period. Of note is the Kamado-zuka mound, named for its innovative fire burial method. This burial practice, which used a kiln-shaped clay-lined burial chamber, involved cremating the body within the chamber and constructing the mound over the remains without relocating the bones. This practice dates to the late 6th to early 7th century. Although initially a unique name for the Kamado-zuka mound in Tōki Senzuka, the term later became a generalized descriptor for similar examples.
The Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group stands out for introducing cremation—a revolutionary burial method in the Japanese archipelago—highlighting the unique characteristics of the community that created these burial mounds.

'''Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group''' (陶器千塚古墳群) [[ja:%E9%99%B6%E5%99%A8%E5%8D%83%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3%E7%BE%A4]] is a collection of [[kofun]] (ancient Kofuns) from the late [[Kofun period]], located in [[Higashiyama]], [[Tōki Kita]], and [[Hirai]] in [[Naka Ward]], [[Sakai City]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. Many of the Kofuns have been destroyed over time.
During the [[Meiji period]], numerous mounds were destroyed through land clearing. However, as of the [[Shōwa era]] (1945–1954), 35 mounds remained, including small [[zenpō-kōen-fun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and predominantly [[empun]] (round mounds). These mounds employed direct burial of wooden coffins, with some, such as Tsuki-zuka, containing two coffins placed adjacently. Excavated artifacts include small bronze mirrors, beads, iron swords, [[Sue ware]], and [[Haji ware]]. One mound included horse harnesses as burial goods, while another example included nuts of the Japanese nutmeg tree placed in a lidded Sue ware dish.
The group is believed to have been formed during the early to late stages of the late Kofun period. Of note is the Kamado-zuka mound, named for its innovative fire burial method. This burial practice, which used a kiln-shaped clay-lined burial chamber, involved cremating the body within the chamber and constructing the mound over the remains without relocating the bones. This practice dates to the late 6th to early 7th century. Although initially a unique name for the Kamado-zuka mound in Tōki Senzuka, the term later became a generalized descriptor for similar examples.
The Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group stands out for introducing cremation—a revolutionary burial method in the Japanese archipelago—highlighting the unique characteristics of the community that created these Kofuns.
== References == * Mori Kōichi, "Tōki Senzuka and Kamado-zuka," *Osaka-fu Shi, Volume 1,* Osaka Prefecture History Compilation Committee, 1978, qq. 911–912.

'''Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group''' (陶器千塚古墳群) [[ja:%E9%99%B6%E5%99%A8%E5%8D%83%E5%A1%9A%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3%E7%BE%A4]] is a collection of [[kofun]] (ancient Kofuns) from the late [[Kofun period]], located in [[Higashiyama]], [[Tōki Kita]], and [[Hirai]] in [[Naka Ward]], [[Sakai City]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. Many of the Kofuns have been destroyed over time.
During the [[Meiji period]], numerous mounds were destroyed through land clearing. However, as of the [[Shōwa era]] (1945–1954), 35 mounds remained, including small [[zenpō-kōen-fun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and predominantly [[empun]] (round mounds). These mounds employed direct burial of wooden coffins, with some, such as Tsuki-zuka, containing two coffins placed adjacently. Excavated artifacts include small bronze mirrors, beads, iron swords, [[Sue pottery]], and [[Haji pottery]]. One mound included horse harnesses as burial goods, while another example included nuts of the Japanese nutmeg tree placed in a lidded Sue pottery dish.
The group is believed to have been formed during the early to late stages of the late Kofun period. Of note is the Kamado-zuka mound, named for its innovative fire burial method. This burial practice, which used a kiln-shaped clay-lined burial chamber, involved cremating the body within the chamber and constructing the mound over the remains without relocating the bones. This practice dates to the late 6th to early 7th century. Although initially a unique name for the Kamado-zuka mound in Tōki Senzuka, the term later became a generalized descriptor for similar examples.
The Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group stands out for introducing cremation—a revolutionary burial method in the Japanese archipelago—highlighting the unique characteristics of the community that created these Kofuns.
== References == * Mori Kōichi, "Tōki Senzuka and Kamado-zuka," *Osaka-fu Shi, Volume 1,* Osaka Prefecture History Compilation Committee, 1978, 11. 911–912.

'''Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group''' (陶器千塚古墳群) is a collection of [[kofun]] (ancient Kofuns) from the late [[Kofun period]], located in [[Higashiyama]], [[Tōki Kita]], and [[Hirai]] in [[Naka Ward]], [[Sakai City]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. Many of the Kofuns have been destroyed over time.
During the [[Meiji period]], numerous mounds were destroyed through land clearing. However, as of the [[Shōwa era]] (1945–1954), 35 mounds remained, including small [[zenpō-kōen-fun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and predominantly [[empun]] (round mounds). These mounds employed direct burial of wooden coffins, with some, such as Tsuki-zuka, containing two coffins placed adjacently. Excavated artifacts include small bronze mirrors, beads, iron swords, [[Sue pottery]], and [[Haji pottery]]. One mound included horse harnesses as burial goods, while another example included nuts of the Japanese nutmeg tree placed in a lidded Sue pottery dish.
The group is believed to have been formed during the early to late stages of the late Kofun period. Of note is the Kamado-zuka mound, named for its innovative fire burial method. This burial practice, which used a kiln-shaped clay-lined burial chamber, involved cremating the body within the chamber and constructing the mound over the remains without relocating the bones. This practice dates to the late 6th to early 7th century. Although initially a unique name for the Kamado-zuka mound in Tōki Senzuka, the term later became a generalized descriptor for similar examples.
The Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group stands out for introducing cremation—a revolutionary burial method in the Japanese archipelago—highlighting the unique characteristics of the community that created these Kofuns.

'''Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group''' (陶器千塚古墳群) is a collection of [[kofun]] (ancient Kofuns) from the late [[Kofun period]], located in [[Higashiyama]], [[Tōki Kita]], and [[Hirai]] in [[Naka Ward]], [[Sakai City]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. Many of the Kofuns have been destroyed over time.
During the [[Meiji period]], numerous mounds were destroyed through land clearing. However, as of the [[Shōwa era]] (1945–1954), 35 mounds remained, including small [[zenpō-kōen-fun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and predominantly [[empun]] (round mounds). These mounds employed direct burial of wooden coffins, with some, such as Tsuki-zuka, containing two coffins placed adjacently. Excavated artifacts include small bronze mirrors, beads, iron swords, [[Sue pottery]], and [[Haji pottery]]. One mound included horse harnesses as burial goods, while another example included nuts of the Japanese nutmeg tree placed in a lidded Sue pottery dish.
The group is believed to have been formed during the early to late stages of the late Kofun period. Of note is the Kamado-zuka mound, named for its innovative fire burial method. This burial practice, which used a kiln-shaped clay-lined burial chamber, involved cremating the body within the chamber and constructing the mound over the remains without relocating the bones. This practice dates to the late 6th to early 7th century. Although initially a unique name for the Kamado-zuka mound in Tōki Senzuka, the term later became a generalized descriptor for similar examples.
The Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group stands out for introducing cremation—a revolutionary burial method in the Japanese archipelago—highlighting the unique characteristics of the community that created these Kofuns.
== References == * Mori Kōichi, "Tōki Senzuka and Kamado-zuka," *Osaka-fu Shi, Volume 1,* Osaka Prefecture History Compilation Committee, 1978, 11. 911–912.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Shinoda Senzuka Kofun Group|ja|信太千塚古墳群|12=draft}}
== External Links == * [http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/city/_bunkazai/_maibun/ntouki-sen29.htm Tōki Senzuka 29th Kofun] - Sakai City Archaeological Site Introduction

'''Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group''' (陶器千塚古墳群) is a collection of [[kofun]] (ancient Kofuns) from the late [[Kofun period]], located in [[Higashiyama]], [[Tōki Kita]], and [[Hirai]] in [[Naka Ward]], [[Sakai City]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. Many of the Kofuns have been destroyed over time.
During the [[Meiji period]], numerous mounds were destroyed through land clearing. However, as of the [[Shōwa era]] (1945–1954), 35 mounds remained, including small [[zenpō-kōen-fun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and predominantly [[empun]] (round mounds). These mounds employed direct burial of wooden coffins, with some, such as Tsuki-zuka, containing two coffins placed adjacently. Excavated artifacts include small bronze mirrors, beads, iron swords, [[Sue pottery]], and [[Haji pottery]]. One mound included horse harnesses as burial goods, while another example included nuts of the Japanese nutmeg tree placed in a lidded Sue pottery dish.
The group is believed to have been formed during the early to late stages of the late Kofun period. Of note is the Kamado-zuka mound, named for its innovative fire burial method. This burial practice, which used a kiln-shaped clay-lined burial chamber, involved cremating the body within the chamber and constructing the mound over the remains without relocating the bones. This practice dates to the late 6th to early 7th century. Although initially a unique name for the Kamado-zuka mound in Tōki Senzuka, the term later became a generalized descriptor for similar examples.
The Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group stands out for introducing cremation—a revolutionary burial method in the Japanese archipelago—highlighting the unique characteristics of the community that created these Kofuns.

One mound included horse harnesses as burial goods, while another example included nuts of the Japanese nutmeg tree placed in a lidded Sue pottery dish.

'''Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group''' (陶器千塚古墳群) is a collection of [[kofun]] (ancient Kofuns) from the late [[Kofun period]], located in [[Higashiyama]], [[Tōki Kita]], and [[Hirai]] in [[Naka Ward]], [[Sakai City]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. Many of the Kofuns have been destroyed over time.

Although initially a unique name for the Kamado-zuka mound in Tōki Senzuka, the term later became a generalized descriptor for similar examples.

13 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:陶器千塚古墳群 up to rev 98937951
revision.text:
'''陶器千塚古墳群'''（とうきせんづかこふんぐん）は、[[大阪府]][[堺市]][[中区 (堺市)|中区]][[東山 (堺市)|東山]]、[[陶器北]]、[[平井 (堺市)|平井]]に分布する[[古墳時代]]後期の[[群集墳]]である。現在ではその多くは消滅している。
== 概要== 陶器千塚古墳群は[[明治]]時代に開墾によって相当数が破壊されている。それでも[[昭和]]20年代（1945年～1954年）には小型の[[前方後円墳]]を含む35基が現存し、その大部分は[[円墳]]であった。木棺直葬の葬方をとり、ツキ塚のように2棺が併置きされたものもあった。出土遺物には、小型銅鏡、玉類、鉄刀、須恵器、土師器などがあり、一基には馬具を[[副葬]]していた。また、須恵器の蓋付の坏の中にカヤの実を入れた例があった。この古墳群は、古墳時代後期初頭から形成が始まり、後期後半に形成を終えているようである。なお陶器千塚にはカマド塚と命名された火葬古墳がある。窯形木心粘土槨とでも呼ぶべき埋葬施設を築き、この内部で遺体を火葬にし、その後[[火葬]]にした骨を動かすことなく、墳丘を築いたもので、6世紀末から7世紀初頭の時期のもののようである。カマド塚と言う名称は陶器千塚のこの火葬古墳の固有名称としてつけられものであるが、その後、この種の類例に一般化して使われるようになったものである。陶器千塚は、火葬という改新的葬方が、日本列島においてひときわ早く出現したところであり、ここにこの古墳群を築いた集団の特色がうかがえるようである。
==参考文献 == *森浩一「陶器千塚とカマド塚」 『大阪府史 第一巻』 大阪府史編纂委員会 1978年 911-912頁
==関連項目 == *[[信太千塚古墳群]]
== 外部リンク== [http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/city/_bunkazai/_maibun/ntouki-sen29.htm 陶器千塚29号墳] 堺の遺跡紹介 堺市
{{デフォルトソート:とうきせんつかこふんくん}} [[category:群集墳]] [[category:大阪府の古墳]] [[category:堺市中区の歴史]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:陶器千塚古墳群]] {{Infobox ancient site}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11658436]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:32, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group''' (陶器千塚古墳群) is a collection of [[kofun]] (ancient Kofuns) from the late [[Kofun period]], located in [[:en:Higashiyama|Higashiyama]], [[Tōki Kita]], and [[:en:Hirai|Hirai]] in [[Naka Ward]], [[:en:Sakai City|Sakai City]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]]. Many of the Kofuns have been destroyed over time.
During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], numerous mounds were destroyed through land clearing. However, as of the [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa era]] (1945–1954), 35 mounds remained, including small [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and predominantly [[Round Kofun|Empun]] (round mounds). These mounds employed direct burial of wooden coffins, with some, such as Tsuki-zuka, containing two coffins placed adjacently. Excavated artifacts include small bronze mirrors, beads, iron swords, [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]], and [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]]. One mound included horse harnesses as burial goods, while another example included nuts of the Japanese nutmeg tree placed in a lidded Sue pottery dish.
The group is believed to have been formed during the early to late stages of the late Kofun period. Of note is the Kamado-zuka mound, named for its innovative fire burial method. This burial practice, which used a kiln-shaped clay-lined burial chamber, involved cremating the body within the chamber and constructing the mound over the remains without relocating the bones. This practice dates to the late 6th to early 7th century. Although initially a unique name for the Kamado-zuka mound in Tōki Senzuka, the term later became a generalized descriptor for similar examples.
The Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group stands out for introducing cremation—a revolutionary burial method in the Japanese archipelago—highlighting the unique characteristics of the community that created these Kofuns.
== References == * Mori Kōichi, "Tōki Senzuka and Kamado-zuka," *Osaka-fu Shi, Volume 1,* Osaka Prefecture History Compilation Committee, 1978, 11. 911–912.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Shinoda Senzuka Kofun Cluster|lt=Shinoda Senzuka Kofun Cluster|lt=Shinoda Senzuka Kofun Group|ja|信太千塚古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinoda Senzuka Kofun Group|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/city/_bunkazai/_maibun/ntouki-sen29.htm Tōki Senzuka 29th Kofun] - Sakai City Archaeological Site Introduction

Many of the Kofuns have been destroyed over time.
During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], numerous mounds were destroyed through land clearing. However, as of the [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa era]] (1945–1954), 35 mounds remained, including small [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and predominantly [[Round Kofun|Empun]] (round mounds). These mounds employed direct burial of wooden coffins, with some, such as Tsuki-zuka, containing two coffins placed adjacently. Excavated artifacts include small bronze mirrors, beads, iron swords, [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]], and [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]]. One mound included horse harnesses as burial goods, while another example included nuts of the Japanese nutmeg tree placed in a lidded Sue pottery dish.
The group is believed to have been formed during the early to late stages of the late Kofun period. Of note is the Kamado-zuka mound, named for its innovative fire burial method. This burial practice, which used a kiln-shaped clay-lined burial chamber, involved cremating the body within the chamber and constructing the mound over the remains without relocating the bones. This practice dates to the late 6th to early 7th century. Although initially a unique name for the Kamado-zuka mound in Tōki Senzuka, the term later became a generalized descriptor for similar examples.
The Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group stands out for introducing cremation—a revolutionary burial method in the Japanese archipelago—highlighting the unique characteristics of the community that created these Kofuns.
== References == * Mori Kōichi, "Tōki Senzuka and Kamado-zuka," *Osaka-fu Shi, Volume 1,* Osaka Prefecture History Compilation Committee, 1978, 11. 911–912.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:陶器千塚古墳群]] {{Infobox ancient site}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11658436]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:32, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group''' (陶器千塚古墳群) is a collection of [[kofun]] (ancient Kofuns) from the late [[Kofun period]], located in [[:en:Higashiyama|Higashiyama]], [[Tōki Kita]], and [[:en:Hirai|Hirai]] in [[Naka Ward]], [[:en:Sakai City|Sakai City]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]]. Many of the Kofuns have been destroyed over time.
During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], numerous mounds were destroyed through land clearing. However, as of the [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa era]] (1945–1954), 35 mounds remained, including small [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and predominantly [[Round Kofun|Empun]] (round mounds). These mounds employed direct burial of wooden coffins, with some, such as Tsuki-zuka, containing two coffins placed adjacently. Excavated artifacts include small bronze mirrors, beads, iron swords, [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]], and [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]]. One mound included horse harnesses as burial goods, while another example included nuts of the Japanese nutmeg tree placed in a lidded Sue pottery dish.
The group is believed to have been formed during the early to late stages of the late Kofun period. Of note is the Kamado-zuka mound, named for its innovative fire burial method. This burial practice, which used a kiln-shaped clay-lined burial chamber, involved cremating the body within the chamber and constructing the mound over the remains without relocating the bones. This practice dates to the late 6th to early 7th century. Although initially a unique name for the Kamado-zuka mound in Tōki Senzuka, the term later became a generalized descriptor for similar examples.
The Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group stands out for introducing cremation—a revolutionary burial method in the Japanese archipelago—highlighting the unique characteristics of the community that created these Kofuns.
== References == * Mori Kōichi, "Tōki Senzuka and Kamado-zuka," *Osaka-fu Shi, Volume 1,* Osaka Prefecture History Compilation Committee, 1978, 11. 911–912.

Although initially a unique name for the Kamado-zuka mound in Tōki Senzuka, the term later became a generalized descriptor for similar examples.
The Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group stands out for introducing cremation—a revolutionary burial method in the Japanese archipelago—highlighting the unique characteristics of the community that created these Kofuns.

The group is believed to have been formed during the early to late stages of the late Kofun period. Of note is the Kamado-zuka mound, named for its innovative fire burial method. This burial practice, which used a kiln-shaped clay-lined burial chamber, involved cremating the body within the chamber and constructing the mound over the remains without relocating the bones. This practice dates to the late 6th to early 7th century. Although initially a unique name for the Kamado-zuka mound in Tōki Senzuka, the term later became a generalized descriptor for similar examples.
The Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group stands out for introducing cremation—a revolutionary burial method in the Japanese archipelago—highlighting the unique characteristics of the community that created these Kofuns.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:陶器千塚古墳群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:陶器千塚古墳群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:陶器千塚古墳群]] {{Infobox ancient site}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11658436]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:32, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group''' (陶器千塚古墳群) is a collection of [[kofun]] (ancient Kofuns) from the late [[Kofun period]], located in {{ill|Higashiyama|en|Higashiyama|WD=Q1617660|lt=Higashiyama}}, [[Tōki Kita]], and {{ill|Hirai|en|Hirai|WD=Q348158|lt=Hirai}} in [[Naka Ward]], [[:en:Sakai City|Sakai City]], {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}. Many of the Kofuns have been destroyed over time.
During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, numerous mounds were destroyed through land clearing. However, as of the {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa era}} (1945–1954), 35 mounds remained, including small {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun}} (keyhole-shaped kofun) and predominantly [[Round Kofun|Empun]] (round mounds). These mounds employed direct burial of wooden coffins, with some, such as Tsuki-zuka, containing two coffins placed adjacently. Excavated artifacts include small bronze mirrors, beads, iron swords, {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}}, and {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery}}. One mound included horse harnesses as burial goods, while another example included nuts of the Japanese nutmeg tree placed in a lidded Sue pottery dish.
The group is believed to have been formed during the early to late stages of the late Kofun period. Of note is the Kamado-zuka mound, named for its innovative fire burial method. This burial practice, which used a kiln-shaped clay-lined burial chamber, involved cremating the body within the chamber and constructing the mound over the remains without relocating the bones. This practice dates to the late 6th to early 7th century. Although initially a unique name for the Kamado-zuka mound in Tōki Senzuka, the term later became a generalized descriptor for similar examples.
The Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group stands out for introducing cremation—a revolutionary burial method in the Japanese archipelago—highlighting the unique characteristics of the community that created these Kofuns.
== References == * Mori Kōichi, "Tōki Senzuka and Kamado-zuka," *Osaka-fu Shi, Volume 1,* Osaka Prefecture History Compilation Committee, 1978, 11. 911–912.

Although initially a unique name for the Kamado-zuka mound in Tōki Senzuka, the term later became a generalized descriptor for similar examples.
The Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group stands out for introducing cremation—a revolutionary burial method in the Japanese archipelago—highlighting the unique characteristics of the community that created these Kofuns.
== References == * Mori Kōichi, "Tōki Senzuka and Kamado-zuka," *Osaka-fu Shi, Volume 1,* Osaka Prefecture History Compilation Committee, 1978, 11. 911–912.

'''Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group''' (陶器千塚古墳群) is a collection of [[kofun]] (ancient Kofuns) from the late [[Kofun period]], located in {{ill|Higashiyama|en|Higashiyama|WD=Q1617660|lt=Higashiyama|1=Higashiyama}}, [[Tōki Kita]], and {{ill|Hirai|en|Hirai|WD=Q348158|lt=Hirai|1=Hirai}} in [[Naka Ward]], [[:en:Sakai City|Sakai City]], {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}. Many of the Kofuns have been destroyed over time.
During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, numerous mounds were destroyed through land clearing. However, as of the {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa era|1=Shōwa era}} (1945–1954), 35 mounds remained, including small {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} (keyhole-shaped kofun) and predominantly [[Round Kofun|Empun]] (round mounds). These mounds employed direct burial of wooden coffins, with some, such as Tsuki-zuka, containing two coffins placed adjacently. Excavated artifacts include small bronze mirrors, beads, iron swords, {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}}, and {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery|1=Haji pottery}}. One mound included horse harnesses as burial goods, while another example included nuts of the Japanese nutmeg tree placed in a lidded Sue pottery dish.
The group is believed to have been formed during the early to late stages of the late Kofun period. Of note is the Kamado-zuka mound, named for its innovative fire burial method. This burial practice, which used a kiln-shaped clay-lined burial chamber, involved cremating the body within the chamber and constructing the mound over the remains without relocating the bones. This practice dates to the late 6th to early 7th century. Although initially a unique name for the Kamado-zuka mound in Tōki Senzuka, the term later became a generalized descriptor for similar examples.
The Tōki Senzuka Kofun Group stands out for introducing cremation—a revolutionary burial method in the Japanese archipelago—highlighting the unique characteristics of the community that created these Kofuns.
